items.front: What vertical separation normally applies up to and including FL410?
items.back: 1,000 feet.

items.front: What basic vertical separation applies above FL410 unless an exception applies?
items.back: 2,000 feet.

items.front: At or above FL290, what separation applies between a non-RVSM aircraft and any other aircraft at or above FL290?
items.back: 2,000 feet.

items.front: In oceanic airspace above FL450, what separation applies between a supersonic aircraft and any other aircraft?
items.back: 4,000 feet.

items.front: Above FL600 between military aircraft, what vertical separation applies?
items.back: 5,000 feet.

items.front: What does the non-RVSM exception change in the normal vertical-separation rule?
items.back: At or above FL290, once a non-RVSM aircraft is involved, 2,000 feet must be applied from all other aircraft at or above FL290.

items.front: At and below FL410, what altitude pattern applies on courses from 0 through 179 degrees?
items.back: Odd cardinal altitudes or flight levels at 2,000-foot intervals.

items.front: At and below FL410, what altitude pattern applies on courses from 180 through 359 degrees?
items.back: Even cardinal altitudes or flight levels at 2,000-foot intervals.

items.front: Below 3,000 feet above the surface, what flight-direction rule applies?
items.back: Any course may be assigned any altitude.

items.front: Above FL410, what eastbound pattern applies?
items.back: Odd cardinal flight levels at 4,000-foot intervals beginning with FL450.

items.front: Above FL410, what westbound pattern applies?
items.back: Odd cardinal flight levels at 4,000-foot intervals beginning with FL430.

items.front: On one-way routes above FL410, what kind of flight level may be assigned?
items.back: Any odd cardinal flight level above FL410.

items.front: When traffic conditions justify a random altitude beyond your facility, what must exist?
items.back: A letter of agreement covering the specific operations and procedures.

items.front: When traffic conditions justify a random altitude that remains within your facility, what approval is needed?
items.back: Prior approval from affected positions or sectors, unless covered by a facility directive.

items.front: When weather justifies the exception, what approvals are needed?
items.back: Prior approval from affected positions or sectors and, if necessary, from the adjacent facility concerned.

items.front: When aircraft operational limitations justify the exception, what extra condition must exist besides coordination?
items.back: The pilot must inform ATC that the available appropriate altitude exceeds the aircraft's operational limitations.

items.front: When do mission requirements justify a random altitude under this paragraph?
items.back: Only when the aircraft is operating on an MTR.

items.front: What special case applies to military aircraft on random routes?
items.back: They may use the exception when prior approval is obtained from the facility concerned.

items.front: When do you use the lowest-usable-flight-level table?
items.back: When a pressure change affects a usable flight level in your area and you must clear aircraft at or above 18,000 feet MSL.

items.front: What is the lowest usable flight level with an altimeter setting of 29.92 or higher?
What is the lowest usable flight level with an altimeter setting from 29.91 through 28.92?
What is the lowest usable flight level with an altimeter setting from 28.91 through 27.92?
When is an adjusted minimum flight level required?
items.back: When the minimum IFR altitude is at or above 18,000 feet MSL and the altimeter setting is below 29.92.

items.front: What adjustment applies with an altimeter setting from 29.91 through 29.42?
items.back: 500 feet.

items.front: What adjustment applies with an altimeter setting from 29.41 through 28.92?
items.back: 1,000 feet.

items.front: What adjustment applies with an altimeter setting from 28.91 through 28.42?
items.back: 1,500 feet.

items.front: What adjustment applies with an altimeter setting from 28.41 through 27.92?
items.back: 2,000 feet.

items.front: What is the basic altitude rule when an MEA exists on the route segment being flown?
items.back: Assign at or above the MEA unless one of the paragraph's specific exceptions applies.

items.front: When may an aircraft be cleared below the MEA but not below the MOCA on the route segment being flown?
items.back: When it remains at least 300 feet above the floor of controlled airspace and one of the listed conditions is met.

items.front: What distance limit applies to the VOR/VORTAC/TACAN below-MEA option?
items.back: It applies only within 22 miles of that NAVAID.

items.front: What must ATC do if radar procedures are used below the MEA and no MOCA is published?
items.back: Assign at or above the MVA or MIA along the route and issue lost communications instructions.

items.front: What equipment alone can satisfy one of the below-MEA exceptions?
items.back: GNSS equipment.

items.front: When may an aircraft be cleared on a jet route below the MEA or above the maximum authorized altitude?
items.back: When radar service is provided and the clearance does not go below the prescribed minimum IFR altitude.

items.front: If a higher MEA begins at a fix and no MCA is specified, when should the climb begin?
items.back: Prior to or immediately after passing the fix where the higher MEA is designated.

items.front: If an MCA is specified, what must ATC ensure?
items.back: The climb begins before the fix so the aircraft crosses the fix at or above the MCA.

items.front: When issuing cruise with an airport clearance limit and an unpublished route, what is normally required?
items.back: An appropriate crossing altitude that ensures IFR obstruction clearance until the aircraft reaches a fix, point, or route where altitude information is available.

items.front: When may a cruise clearance to an airport without a published instrument approach be issued without a crossing restriction?
items.back: When the airport has no published instrument approach procedure.

items.front: When does a climb or descent restriction require a UTC time check?
items.back: When the restriction uses a clock time, except when the clearance is issued by CPDLC or by elapsed time in radar contact with direct two-way VHF/UHF voice communication.

items.front: What does a crossing altitude do to a descent at pilot's discretion?
items.back: It authorizes descent at pilot's discretion for the portion of flight to which the crossing restriction applies.

items.front: When canceling a pilot's-discretion portion by assigning a new altitude, what must the controller say?
items.back: Identify the new altitude as an amended altitude.

items.front: What phraseology is used for a basic crossing restriction at or above an altitude?
items.back: Cross (fix, waypoint) at or above (altitude).

items.front: What phraseology is used for direct-to plus descend-via when the direct fix has no published altitude?
items.back: Proceed direct (fix or waypoint), cross (fix or waypoint) at (altitude), then descend via (STAR name and number).

items.front: After an interim altitude on a SID, what instruction is used when ATC can clear the aircraft back to the published top altitude?
items.back: Climb via SID.

items.front: If ATC needs a different top altitude while retaining published restrictions on the SID, what phraseology is used?
items.back: Climb via SID except maintain (altitude).

items.front: What phraseology is used when a climb-via or descend-via clearance must be restated after clearing the aircraft direct to a waypoint or fix?
items.back: Cleared or proceed direct (waypoint or fix), then climb via SID, or the equivalent descend-via restatement.

items.front: If practicable, what should be told to an aircraft when a climb or descent cannot yet be issued?
items.back: When to expect higher or lower, or when to request the altitude change from another facility.

items.front: What phraseology is used to advise an aircraft of an expected climb or descent in miles or minutes?
items.back: Expect higher or lower in (number of miles or minutes) miles or minutes.

items.front: What alternate advisory may be used instead of an expect-higher or expect-lower message?
items.back: Request altitude or flight-level change from (facility) at the specified time, fix, or altitude.

items.front: Why is this paragraph operationally useful?
items.back: It lets the pilot plan ahead for a climb, descent, or handoff-driven altitude request when the clearance cannot be issued immediately.

items.front: When should altitude confirmation normally be requested in nonradar operations?
items.back: On initial contact and when position reports are received, unless one of the listed exceptions applies.

items.front: What does initial contact mean here?
items.back: The pilot's first radio contact with each sector or position.

items.front: What is one exception to the initial-contact altitude-verification requirement?
items.back: A terminal aircraft transferred from another sector or position within the same facility.

items.front: What must controllers in the USA do with all pilot altitude readbacks?
items.back: Reconfirm them.

items.front: What phraseology is used when verifying a flight level at or above the lowest usable flight level?
items.back: Verify assigned flight level (flight level).

items.front: What phraseology is used when a correct altitude readback is reconfirmed?
items.back: Affirmative (altitude).
